title: Fig and walnut cake
subtitle: A dense, autumnal traybake with dried figs, walnuts and cinnamon
excerpt: Dried figs are soaked briefly in hot water before going into the batter, which keeps them tender and jammy through the bake. Walnuts add crunch and a slight bitterness that balances the sweetness of the figs. This is a traybake format that cuts into squares, keeps well for three days and improves slightly on the second. It bakes in about 35 minutes.
sourceNotes: Figs and walnuts are paired together in confectionery and baking across the Mediterranean, Middle East and Caucasus, particularly in Georgia, Turkey and southern Italy, where both grow in abundance. The combination appears in Elizabeth David's 'Italian Food' (1954) and in Richard Olney's 'Simple French Food' (1974). The traybake format here is a standard British approach.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 traybake (20 x 30 cm), cuts into 12 to 16 pieces
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The traybake keeps well for 3 days at room temperature, wrapped. The fig flavour deepens on the second day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; cover with foil loosely for the final 10 minutes if top is darkening

glossaryTerms.definition: Covering chopped dried fruit with hot water or another liquid for 15 to 20 minutes before adding it to a cake batter. Dried fruit contains very little residual moisture; if added dry, it absorbs moisture from the surrounding batter as the cake bakes, which creates dry patches and an uneven texture in the crumb. Pre-soaked fruit stays tender throughout baking and releases a small amount of liquid that carries sweetness and flavour into the crumb. Drain the soaking liquid away before using unless the recipe specifies otherwise.

content.text: before adding it to the batter. Dry figs added directly will pull moisture from the crumb and create a tight, dry texture around each piece. A 20-minute soak in hot water makes them soft and jammy.

Use a soft brown sugar rather than caster sugar for a slightly more complex, treacly sweetness that pairs better with the figs and cinnamon. The walnuts should be roughly chopped, not finely ground: you want pieces large enough to give a distinct crunch in each bite.
Put the 200 g chopped figs in a bowl, pour over 80 ml boiling water and leave for 20 minutes. Drain away the liquid.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 20 x 30 cm baking tin. Beat the 150 g butter and 175 g soft brown sugar together until combined; it will not be as pale and fluffy as with caster sugar. Beat in the 3 eggs and 1 tsp vanilla. Fold in the 200 g flour, 1.5 tsp baking powder and 1 tsp cinnamon. Stir in the drained figs and 100 g walnuts.

Spread the batter evenly into the prepared tin. Bake for 30 to 38 minutes until the top is deep golden brown with firm edges, and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. If the top darkens before the centre is set, lay a sheet of foil loosely over the tin. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then lift out onto a rack before cutting into squares.

items.problem: Dry, tough patches around the fig pieces
items.solution: The figs were not soaked before adding to the batter. Dry figs draw moisture aggressively from the surrounding crumb during baking. Even a brief 15-minute soak makes a large difference to the final texture.

items.problem: Walnuts on the surface burnt before the cake is baked through
items.solution: Fold any surface walnuts down into the batter before loading into the oven. Alternatively, cover the tin with foil for the first 20 minutes of baking, then remove it for the final 10 to 15 minutes to allow the top to colour.

items.problem: Batter is very stiff and hard to spread
items.solution: The soft brown sugar and soaked figs both add density. Add 2 tablespoons of milk to loosen the batter to a spreadable consistency. This will not affect the outcome.
